claudeCode.summary: The --channels preview (v2.1.80-81) opens a new surface for mobile-first agent interaction — but it's raw and undocumented. Skills ecosystem is heating up (Sahil Lavingia open-sourced his), yet there's no discovery or quality layer. Memory optimisations (~100MB saved) signal scaling pain that's been quietly accumulating.

items.title: Skills ecosystem has no discovery or curation layer
items.snippet: Sahil Lavingia open-sourced his Claude Code skills. Multiple Show HN posts sharing personal skills. Growing ecosystem with no marketplace, no ratings, no search. Opportunity: a skills directory with quality signals would own the distribution layer.

items.title: --channels is powerful but has no UX for non-developers
items.snippet: MCP servers can now push messages into sessions and relay tool approvals to your phone. Massive potential for mobile-first AI workflows — but requires technical setup. Opportunity: a no-code channels configurator or mobile companion app.

items.title: Productivity patterns are tribal knowledge, not product
items.snippet: Blog post on Claude Code productivity patterns hit 19 points. Users are figuring out workflows individually through blog posts and HN threads. Opportunity: onboarding flows or guided templates that encode best practices into the product.

items.title: Large repo performance still a pain point despite optimisations
items.snippet: Two consecutive releases cut ~100MB of startup memory. This was worth shipping twice = significant user complaints. Opportunity: repo-aware context management — smart indexing so the tool doesn't need to load everything upfront.

items.title: Multi-agent orchestration emerging from the community
items.snippet: Show HN: agents chatting by typing in each other's terminals. Plus 'Outworked' — an office UI for Claude Code agents. Users want multi-agent workflows but are building them with duct tape. Opportunity: first-party multi-agent orchestration.

openClaw.summary: The 384-point security audit is an existential product problem — 7.1% of skills have critical flaws and OAuth tokens sit unencrypted. Architecture is elegant but the trust layer is missing. Meanwhile, competitors like NemoClaw and a 10MB Go alternative are emerging, signalling the ecosystem is fragmenting before it's mature.

items.title: Security trust layer is completely absent
items.snippet: 384-point HN post. 7.1% of 3,984 skills expose credentials. Unencrypted OAuth tokens. Prompt injection across messaging inputs. Opportunity: a verified skills marketplace with sandboxing and credential vaulting would be the #1 trust differentiator.

items.title: Memory system being designed in the open — context vs knowledge split
items.snippet: New memory plugin separates context engineering from knowledge lifecycle. Introduces memory typing and structured extraction. Opportunity: whoever solves persistent agent memory with accuracy guarantees wins the long-running agent use case.

items.title: Ecosystem fragmenting — 10MB Go alternative already supports full skills
items.snippet: Capabot reimplements OpenClaw in Go at 10MB with full Clawhub skills compatibility. Users want the ecosystem without the bloat. Opportunity: lightweight runtimes that are plugin-compatible could commoditise the agent layer — the value shifts to the skills marketplace.

items.title: NemoClaw comparison reveals positioning gap
items.snippet: Head-to-head with NVIDIA's NemoClaw. OpenClaw wins on plugin breadth, NemoClaw on enterprise trust. Opportunity: OpenClaw needs a clear enterprise story — or it becomes the hobbyist default while NemoClaw takes the budget.

items.title: Core architecture is simple — but no one knows that
items.snippet: Architecture review shows 5 core components, buildable in a weekend. 7 points on HN = high interest. Opportunity: better developer onboarding and architecture docs would accelerate contributor growth — the project's moat is community, not complexity.
